codeReview.summary: Implementation is complete and correct. All five spec requirements are met: HealthResponse type updated, pure mapHealthResponseToState helper extracted to health.ts, Header.svelte polls fetchHealth on mount and on chain change with 30-second interval and cleanup, CSS indicator styles match spec, and indicator is placed first in .header-actions. Tests cover all four mapping branches (ok+synced=true, ok+synced=false, ok+synced=null, ok+synced=undefined, non-ok→error). All validators passed: 85 frontend tests, svelte-check clean, Vite build successful, live browser verification confirmed blue ● indicator in the correct position.

issues.description: Double startHealthPolling on mount: both onMount and $effect call startHealthPolling(chain) on component initialization, causing two immediate health fetches and the onMount-created interval being immediately stopped and replaced. startHealthPolling always calls stopHealthPolling() first so no interval leaks, but the redundant initial fetch is wasteful. The idiomatic Svelte 5 approach would be to use only $effect (which fires on mount and on change) and remove the startHealthPolling call from onMount, keeping only the cleanup return in onMount.

The explicit 'chain;' read at the top of $effect is redundant: startHealthPolling(chain) on the following line already reads the reactive chain prop, which is sufficient to register the dependency. Harmless, but could be confusing.

No AbortController passed to fetchHealth when the interval fires. If a health request is in-flight when startHealthPolling is called again (chain change or re-mount), the old request is not cancelled. A stale response from the old chain could transiently set healthState after the chain has changed. fetchHealth already accepts an optional signal parameter (see api.ts:98). The race window is small since health checks complete quickly, and the next poll corrects state, but a per-request abort signal would be cleaner.

The catch block in updateHealth (fetch failure → healthState = 'error') is not unit-tested. Tests cover mapHealthResponseToState comprehensively, which satisfies the spec's 'tests verifying health state mapping logic' requirement, but a brief test for the async error path (or a note that it's covered by browser verification) would improve confidence. Not a spec gap.

sharedStateObservations.observation: frontend-worker SKILL.md (Step 7: Manual Verification) says to start the dev server at port 5173 ('cd frontend && npm run dev (port 5173)'), but services.yaml defines the vite-dev service on port 5199. The worker attempted port 5199 (from services.yaml), hit a conflict, tried --strictPort on 5199 (failed), then succeeded on 5205. Three tool calls were spent finding a working port. The skill should either reference services.yaml for the canonical port, or note that the port in vite.config.ts may override the default.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: SKILL.md step 7 says 'port 5173'. services.yaml vite-dev.port = 5199. Transcript shows: vite --port 5199 (started but crashed), vite --port 5199 --strictPort (failed), vite --port 5205 --strictPort (succeeded). Worker read vite.config.ts between the second and third attempts.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The fetchHealth function in api.ts uses the global FETCH_TIMEOUT_MS constant (300_000 ms / 5 minutes) — appropriate for long-running price queries but excessive for a health-check poller that runs every 30 seconds. Keeping a 5-minute timeout for health checks means a hanging server could accumulate up to 10 in-flight health requests before any of them timeout. This pattern (shared timeout constant across all fetch calls) isn't documented in AGENTS.md or library/architecture.md. A convention note recommending shorter timeouts for polling calls, or an API-specific timeout override, would help future workers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: frontend/src/lib/api.ts:11 — FETCH_TIMEOUT_MS = 300_000. frontend/src/lib/api.ts:96-100 — fetchHealth calls fetchWithTimeout with no custom timeout. frontend/src/lib/components/Header.svelte:22 — updateHealth calls fetchHealth without a signal or custom timeout.

summary: PASS. All required functionality is implemented and verified: HealthResponse type extended, mapHealthResponseToState pure helper with full test coverage, 30-second polling with immediate fetch on mount and chain change, proper interval cleanup, correct indicator rendering (●/▲/✕) as first element in header-actions, and matching CSS. Four non-blocking issues noted: redundant double startHealthPolling call on mount (onMount + $effect), a redundant 'chain;' dependency read, no AbortController for stale in-flight requests on chain change, and no unit test for the async error catch path. None are blocking. Two shared-state observations: skill port mismatch (5173 vs 5199) caused wasted tool calls during verification; and the shared 5-minute fetch timeout is undocumented and sub-optimal for health polling.
